content:
John Baez said:

My favorite words today:

liquescent

quilisma

This reminds me. Once I was writing a bunch of string diagrams with my fountain pen with lots of Toffoli gates and someone thought I was writing a musical score!

Musical notation has always been a mystery for me. I studied solfege and I can read it, but I'm increadibly slow. I've always considered it a very cumbersome and inefficient way of writing music, and I'm always surprised at how many people can read it with ease...
I'm very crappy at reading music, probably because I never practice: I mainly improvise on the piano rather than trying to play sheet music.

John Baez said:

I'm very crappy at reading music, probably because I never practice: I mainly improvise on the piano rather than trying to play sheet music.

For me it is the opposite, I mainly play classical music. I think the reason for my crappyness is that I'm fast at memorizing music once I play it, so I almost never have to rely on reading while playing. And so my reading is crap...
I used to take classes for classical guitar and had to read sheet music and it was so hard. Now I've picked up classical guitar again, but just use guitar tabs. It makes it so much more pleasant.

John van de Wetering said:

I used to take classes for classical guitar and had to read sheet music and it was so hard. Now I've picked up classical guitar again, but just use guitar tabs. It makes it so much more pleasant.

Same for me with piano, I learned solfege just because my teacher insisted on it :smile:

Fabrizio Genovese said:

Musical notation has always been a mystery for me

I can't imagine anything very different to it working. It's pretty much a slightly more information dense version of a piano roll / sequencer grid (where note duration is encoded rather than being analogue by the physical length of the note)
The thing that causes me the most problems is when notes go too above or below the line
also, I'm really bad at distinguishing on which row a note is.
If the height of a note would be encoded with a symbol and not spacially, that would already work much better for me

You should read intervals, not notes. It's easy to distinguish intervals visually up to a tenth or so--the lines and spaces give you the parity of the interval directly, so you only need to distinguish a handful of distances.
It's much more important to distinguish fifths from sixths than Gs from As, because the whole musical system is symmetric in the pitch classes anyways.

Fabrizio Genovese said:

John Baez said:

I'm very crappy at reading music, probably because I never practice: I mainly improvise on the piano rather than trying to play sheet music.

For me it is the opposite, I mainly play classical music. I think the reason for my crappyness is that I'm fast at memorizing music once I play it, so I almost never have to rely on reading while playing. And so my reading is crap...

Actually it may not be the exact opposite. I'm pretty good at making up melodies and harmonies so I find reading music to be boring in comparison. I wish I were good at reading music, but it's never at the top of my priorities when I sit down at the piano.

However, I'm not good at memorizing exact note sequences; I'm better at remembering which kinds of harmonies convey which emotions.

On the topic of sheet music, in case any of you are not aware, this is an excellent resource for public domain sheet music: https://imslp.org/wiki/Main_Page
I get most of the music I play from IMSLP these days.
There actually are some systems of shapes with notes, to aid with visual distinguishment, but they’re obscure
“Shape notes have also been called character notes and patent notes, respectfully, and buckwheat notes and dunce notes, pejoratively.” from Wikipedia
